result.chapter_summary: Hirsch argues that language and prose history exhibit a 'progressive' tendency toward greater communicative efficiency, moving toward maximum effect with minimum cognitive effort. By examining the evolution of grapholects like English and broader Indo-European families, he posits that linguistic change is a trial-and-error process that gradually codifies more functional forms.

claims.text: An historical perspective on prose composition yields principles which transcend historical and cultural contingencies.
claims.source_passage: I have chosen nonetheless to venture into this difficult terrain, because only an historical perspective on the subject of prose composition promises to yield principles which transcend historical and cultural contingencies.

claims.text: The inherent ideology of literacy can be discovered in the history of literacy.
claims.source_passage: In the Introduction I asserted that the inherent ideology of literacy can be discovered in the history of literacy

claims.text: The main goals of teaching composition are disclosed in certain irresistible tendencies of linguistic change.
claims.source_passage: the main goals of teaching composition are disclosed in certain irresistible tendencies of linguistic change.

claims.text: Language change is on the whole progressive.
claims.source_passage: Other twentieth-century linguists, most notably Jespersen, and most recently Martinet, hold the view that language change is on the whole progressive. This last view seems to me the correct one

claims.text: Language change is not progressive in every respect or in every instance.
claims.source_passage: I must quickly state, as do all proponents of the progressive view, that language change is not progressive in every respect or in every instance.

claims.text: The primary direction of linguistic change is toward ever greater communicative functionality.
claims.source_passage: The respect in which the tendency of historical change has been forward is in its movement towards ever greater communicative functionality.

claims.text: The languages for which a continuous record exists have moved toward increased communicative efficiency.
claims.source_passage: the evidence seems to me irresistible that the languages of which we possess a continuous record have indeed moved on the whole towards increased communicative efficiency.

claims.text: Prose has changed in the direction of greater communicative efficiency.
claims.source_passage: it would not be surprising to find that one special domain of language—prose—has also changed in the direction of greater communicative efficiency.

claims.text: The best linguistic expression is that which produces maximum effect in the hearer with minimum effort from the speaker.
claims.source_passage: “In valuation of a language or a linguistic expression, both sides should be taken into consideration: the best is what with a minimum of effort on the part of the speaker produces a maximum of effect in the hearer.”

claims.text: Indo-European languages show increased functionality through shorter forms that involve less muscular exertion.
claims.source_passage: 1. The forms are generally shorter, thus involving less muscular exertion and requiring less time for their enunciation.

claims.text: Indo-European languages show increased functionality through greater regularity in form and syntactic use.
claims.source_passage: 3. Their formation is much more regular. 4. Their syntactic use also presents fewer irregularities.

claims.text: Analytic and abstract linguistic characters enable combinations and constructions that were formerly impossible.
claims.source_passage: Their more analytic and abstract character renders possible a great many combinations and constructions which were formerly impossible.

claims.text: English prose has progressed in efficiency as an instrument of expression, paralleling the progressive history of the English language.
claims.source_passage: Now if a language like English has grown more efficient 'as an instrument of expression,' it is reasonable to guess that English prose has also progressed in the same respect

claims.text: The disappearance of superfluous inflections increased English's capacity for vigorous condensation.
claims.source_passage: The disappearance of superfluous inflections... have greatly increased the capacity of the language for vigorous condensation.

claims.text: Linguistic change is governed by a process of trial and error.
claims.source_passage: it is highly reasonable to conceive of linguistic change as governed by a process of trial and error.

claims.text: English has gained in efficiency as a means of expression through the acquisition of new machinery and the loss of superfluous inflections.
claims.source_passage: English has gained in efficiency as a means of expression. The disappearance of superfluous inflections, and the reduction of those which remain to mere consonantal suffixes, which in most instances do not add a syllable, have greatly increased the capacity of the language for vigorous condensation.

claims.text: The tendency toward shorter linguistic forms and grammatical simplification is a universal fact of linguistic history.
claims.source_passage: the tendency to shorter forms and grammatical simplification is 'a universal fact of linguistic history'

claims.text: The most efficient forms of expression are slowly developed and codified rather than arising fully-formed at the start of a language.
claims.source_passage: Why should the most efficient forms of expression have arisen full-blown from the start? It is inherently more probable that they should have been slowly developed and codified.

claims.text: Linguistic communities discard redundancies when they discover ways to secure meaning without them.
claims.source_passage: if a way were discovered for securing meaning without these redundancies, then people would tend to adopt it and discard the redundancies.

claims.text: The universal tendency toward linguistic efficiency is caused by a human universal to minimize time and effort to produce the same effect.
claims.source_passage: The tendency to greater linguistic efficiency is universal because mankind is universally lazy and impatient. It is a human universal to minimize time and effort in order to produce the same effect.

claims.text: There is a definitive inverse relationship between linguistic complexity and frequency of use.
claims.source_passage: establish beyond doubt an inverse relationship between linguistic complexity and frequency of use.

claims.text: The most-used words in a language tend to be its shortest words.
claims.source_passage: The most-used words of a language tend to be its shortest words. When television is used often, it becomes TV (USA) or telly (Brit.).

claims.text: Prose is generally more representative of normal oral speech than poetry.
claims.source_passage: And since prose is generally more representative of normal oral speech than is poetry, it is reasonable to guess that the history of prose must parallel the progressive history of the language as a whole.

claims.text: The history of prose is significantly more difficult to trace than the grammatical and lexical history of a language.
claims.source_passage: one must nonetheless concede that it is far more difficult to trace the history of prose in English or French or German than it is to trace the grammatical and lexical history of those languages.

claims.text: There is currently no satisfactory account of the history of English prose due to scholar disagreement.
claims.source_passage: we lack, so far as I can discover, a single satisfactory account of the history of English prose. Scholars who have turned their attention to the subject disagree strikingly in their views on the nature and pattern of that history.

claims.text: English prose style exhibits a wavy, uncertain pattern over time rather than the linear progression seen in language evolution.
claims.source_passage: The history of prose style appears to form a wavy, uncertain pattern, unlike the more linear course of the English language as a whole.

claims.text: The chaotic state of prose history studies stems from the methodological focus on individual texts rather than statistical overviews.
claims.source_passage: The reason for scholarly disarray in the history of prose, as contrasted with scholarly orderliness in the history of the language, may be sought in the kinds of questions asked by the two different inquiries. The history of a language is a statistical overview; the history of prose style examines individual texts.

claims.text: Rigorous studies of English prose have failed to establish historical patterns because they prioritize individual authors.
claims.source_passage: The more rigorous studies of English prose style have concerned themselves with individual authors rather than with the historical pattern of prose taken as a whole.

claims.text: The history of a language is characterized by scholarly orderliness, whereas the history of prose is characterized by scholarly disarray.
claims.source_passage: The reason for scholarly disarray in the history of prose, as contrasted with scholarly orderliness in the history of the language, may be sought in the kinds of questions asked by the two different inquiries.

claims.text: The history of language is a statistical overview, while the history of prose style focuses on individual texts.
claims.source_passage: The history of a language is a statistical overview; the history of prose style examines individual texts.

claims.text: The uncertainties in the history of prose are inherent to the subject itself rather than just the result of imprecise scholarly questions.
claims.source_passage: I do not mean to imply that the history of prose would yield more satisfactory results if scholars asked better, more precise questions. The reasons for the uncertainties that beset the subject lie in the subject itself.

claims.text: No stylistic generalization regarding sentence length or word order holds without exception for every English writer from the sixteenth to the twentieth century.
claims.source_passage: If one asked, for instance, how English prose changed in sentence length and word order, one could hardly make a single generalization which held without exception for every writer.

claims.text: Syntactic deaths and births have been exceedingly rare in the English language over the past five centuries.
claims.source_passage: I have not been able to find a single clear-cut example of such discontinuity—a result which at least suggests that syntactic deaths and births must be exceedingly rare over the past five centuries.

claims.text: The authority of canonical writers and widely known books like the Bible preserves syntactic forms in prose that have become defunct in oral speech.
claims.source_passage: Afterwards, the authority of such canonical writers as Jonson and Shakespeare will encourage later writers to use syntactic devices which may have become quite defunct in the oral speech of their own time.

claims.text: The 'self-conscious craft' of writing makes the history of prose more difficult to manage than the general history of language.
claims.source_passage: The interposition of self-conscious craft in writing makes the history of prose much more difficult to manage than the general history of language.

claims.text: In all long-established grapholects, both language and prose progress towards greater regularity of syntax.
claims.source_passage: Universally, in all the long-established grapholects, both the history of language and the history of prose progress towards greater regularity of syntax.

claims.text: The number of frequently used syntactic patterns in European grapholects has reduced in modern times, except in self-consciously individualistic writing.
claims.source_passage: Except in self-consciously individualistic writing, the number of frequently used syntactic patterns has become much reduced in modern times in all the grapholects of Europe.

claims.text: The scope for choice in word order has diminished since the Elizabethan era.
claims.source_passage: Except in the most contrived writing—which is statistically rare in any case—the scope for choice in word order has diminished since Shakespeare’s day.

claims.text: The normalization of syntax has made effective prose composition easier today than it was in Shakespeare's time, at least at the level of the sentence.
claims.source_passage: For us, the problem of effective prose composition is a great deal easier than it was for Shakespeare, at least at the level of the sentence.

claims.text: Increased regularity of syntax makes language more efficient as an instrument of expression by reducing the range of expectancy and uncertainty in both production and reception.
claims.source_passage: Increased regularity of syntax, regardless of the system adopted, in itself makes language more efficient “as an instrument of expression” by reducing the range of expectancy and uncertainty both in the production and in the reception of speech.

claims.text: Modern prose is a more functional instrument than the prose of the past because of syntactic normalization.
claims.source_passage: This normalization of syntax alone makes modern prose a more functional instrument than the prose of the past.

claims.text: The functional superiority of modern prose is an absolute claim grounded in universal human psychology rather than cultural bias.
claims.source_passage: To defend this assertion in absolute terms, as transcending cultural and historical bias, it will be necessary to ground it in certain absolutes of human psychology which transcend not only cultural eras but also the diverse types of human languages.

claims.text: The claim that modern prose is superior in functionality must be grounded in universal psychological absolutes that transcend culture and specific languages.
claims.source_passage: To defend this assertion in absolute terms, as transcending cultural and historical bias, it will be necessary to ground it in certain absolutes of human psychology which transcend not only cultural eras but also the diverse types of human languages.

claims.text: The difficulty of writing good prose arises largely from the linguistic abnormality of addressing a monologue to an unseen and unknown audience.
claims.source_passage: I further assume that the difficulty of writing good prose arises very largely from the linguistic abnormality of addressing a monologue to an unseen and unknown audience.

claims.text: The invention of printing made prose writing increasingly important for an increasingly large audience after the fifteenth century.
claims.source_passage: the invention of printing made prose writing increasingly important for an increasingly large audience after the fifteenth century.

claims.text: Sixteenth-century English prose writers primarily modeled their work on the prose of other languages and the oral techniques of public speakers.
claims.source_passage: we can guess that the sixteenth century post-Gutenberg writers of English prose took their chief models from two sources: from the prose of authors in other languages and from the oral techniques of public speakers whose monologic utterances before large audiences were necessarily governed by some of the same constraints and requirements which govern communication in writing.

claims.text: Early prose writers who used ordinary conversation as their model wrote less effectively than those who used the model of public oration.
claims.source_passage: The misguided group of early prose writers who supposed their proper model to be ordinary conversation would have written less effectively than those authors who adopted the model of public oration.

claims.text: Early sixteenth-century writers needed to invent specific syntactic and lexical techniques to provide clues to meaning in the absence of an interpersonal context.
claims.source_passage: the early sixteenth-century practitioners would need to invent special syntactic and lexical techniques which would help supply clues to meaning in the absence of an interpersonal context.

claims.text: Prose conventions were established through a process of trial and error, where effective experiments were chosen and ineffective ones neglected.
claims.source_passage: Gradually, these early writers would learn from one another, choosing those conventions which seemed to work well and neglecting those which worked badly. By this process of trial and error, syntactic and lexical devices would become gradually established.

claims.text: The regularization of writing conventions inherently makes the techniques more communicative than they were before normalization.
claims.source_passage: This subsequent regularization of writing conventions would in itself make the techniques more communicative than they were before the normalization occurred. For normalization in itself contributes greatly to communicative effectiveness.

claims.text: The pattern of development toward communicative efficiency likely occurred in every European grapholect between the sixteenth and eighteenth centuries.
claims.source_passage: I would conjecture that the prose of every country of Europe probably followed this general pattern of development between the sixteenth and eighteenth centuries.

claims.text: Any specific genre of eighteenth-century prose is more uniform in style than the same genre in the sixteenth century.
claims.source_passage: Any genre of prose in the eighteenth century will be found to be more uniform in style than a similar genre of the sixteenth century.

claims.text: By the eighteenth century, prose exhibits much more uniformity of practice compared to earlier periods.
claims.source_passage: By the eighteenth century, we should expect to find much more uniformity of practice.

claims.text: More communicative linguistic techniques tend to expel less effective techniques over time.
claims.source_passage: The more communicative techniques will have tended to expel the less effective ones, and those techniques which have predominated will also have become regularized.

claims.text: English prose history follows a pattern of progressive communicative efficiency.
claims.source_passage: But surely no one will object that the pattern I have traced is incorrect for English prose.

claims.text: Psycholinguistic testing provides objective criteria for comparing the communicative efficiency of different prose passages.
claims.source_passage: As I shall mention when discussing the psycholinguistic testing of readability in prose, there are ways of making quite objective comparisons between passages like these, on the criterion of communicative efficiency.

claims.text: The subjective reading experience is a useful, if imperfect, guide for detecting reductions in reading time and effort.
claims.source_passage: Most readers will instead consult their own subjective experience of the passages—by no means a sure guide, but a useful one nonetheless, for grasping the progressive reduction of time and effort required to read the three passages.

claims.text: Syntactic techniques are more crucial for determining the efficiency of prose than lexical choices or verb forms.
claims.source_passage: For our purposes, the devices that are more crucial than lexical choices, or even verb forms, are the syntactic techniques in Painter.

claims.text: Certain lexical choices in early translations (like Painter's) became obsolete due to their marginal inefficiencies.
claims.source_passage: Justice requires us to exclude from consideration some of the lexical choices in Painter which history has rendered obsolete because of their marginal inefficiencies.

claims.text: Syntactic reordering can increase efficiency by overcoming structural ambiguities.
claims.source_passage: It would be much more efficient to write: from base estate, the same advanced him to be Sultan of Babylon. That is because the altered syntax overcomes an amb

claims.text: Reordering syntax can overcome ambiguities regarding the closeness of grammatical-semantic pairing.
claims.source_passage: That is because the altered syntax overcomes an ambiguity about the closeness of grammatical-semantic pairing in the original.

claims.text: Separating the subject and verb with an unresolved modification holds the reader in a state of semantic-syntactic uncertainty.
claims.source_passage: by separating the subject and verb and inserting a not-yet-resolved modification between them, the writer holds the reader in semantic-syntactic uncertainty for a longer time

claims.text: The speedy fulfillment of semantic-syntactic expectations is a central principle of readability.
claims.source_passage: These two principles—the speedy resolution of semantic-syntactic ambiguity and the speedy fulfillment of semantic-syntactic expectations— will prove to be central principles of readability.

claims.text: Requiring a reader to hold multiple subject-modifications in mind before reaching the main clause increases mental effort.
claims.source_passage: This arrangement requires a good deal of mental effort by the reader, who must hold in mind all these subject-modifications before their connection with the main clause becomes clear.

claims.text: A writer can use tacit temporal and causal logic to make complex sentences readable even when the syntax is inefficient.
claims.source_passage: That this translation should be nonetheless fairly readable reflects a remarkable virtuosity in the translator, who makes the sentence work by introducing a tacit temporal and causal logic into the sequence.

claims.text: Proleptic conventions in English prose were still unsettled and inconsistent in the early 17th century.
claims.source_passage: From this usage we can infer a still unsettled system of proleptic conventions, illustrated further by the now rare (in the sense used) so powerful as, rather than the more normal form used in both the sixteenth- and eighteenth-century versions: so great that.

claims.text: By the 18th century, English prose had adopted normalized proleptic conventions that match present-day usage.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, in the eighteenth-century version I cannot find a single proleptic device that fails to follow the present-day normalized conventions: so-that; both-and; from-to; plus a proleptic indication of temporal sequence—at last.

claims.text: The use of proleptic devices and parallel forms increases readability by preparing and fulfilling reader expectations.
claims.source_passage: The phrasing prepares and fulfills reader expectations by the use of both proleptic devices and parallel forms.

claims.text: Modern translations are generally easier to read than historical translations of the same work.
claims.source_passage: while very recent translations will be the briskest and easiest to read of all.

claims.text: Comparative readability is vulnerable to the objection that readability depends entirely on the linguistic expectations of a specific historical period.
claims.source_passage: The historicist can quite reasonably object that the comparative readability of a translation will depend upon the linguistic expectations of readers, expectations which differ in different periods.

claims.text: Further passages from successive translations of The Decameron will consistently show that more recent translations are easier to read.
claims.source_passage: The common sense of the reader will foresee that further passages from the three translations of The Decameron will yield the same characteristic results.

claims.text: A mannered translation of the late nineteenth century might be less readable than one from 1741 despite being more recent.
claims.source_passage: He might even foresee that a mannered translation of the late nineteenth century might be less readable than that of 1741

claims.text: Psychological constants operate in the human mind across all historical times and geographical places.
claims.source_passage: where I discuss some psychological constants which operate in the human mind in all times and places.

claims.text: Psychological universals make it possible to conduct valid comparative readability tests on texts from different languages and eras.
claims.source_passage: On the basis of these psychological universals it is possible to conduct tests on and make comparative inferences from texts written in different languages and taken from different eras.

claims.text: Criteria for judging prose are not absolute imperatives but are chosen based on the evaluator's purposes, values, or tastes.
claims.source_passage: The criteria by which one judges prose are not sanctioned by any absolute imperatives. The criteria have to be chosen according to one’s purposes, values, or tastes.

claims.text: The norm of communicative efficiency reflects the actual historical direction of both oral and written speech evolution.
claims.source_passage: I am aware that the norm of communicative efficiency, even though it reflects the historical directions of oral and written speech, is not a norm that commends itself to everyone’s taste.

claims.text: The majority of people prefer to achieve a communicative effect with the least expenditure of effort.
claims.source_passage: Most people do prefer to achieve the same effect in the most economical way, with the least expenditure of effort.

claims.text: A historically accurate definition of communicative efficiency must emphasize the sameness of communicative intentions between compared texts.
claims.source_passage: A historically accurate definition of communicative efficiency will therefore lay great stress on the sameness of the communicative intentions being compared in two different texts.

claims.text: Two different texts can carry identical communicative intentions.
claims.source_passage: And I have argued elsewhere in great detail the theoretical and empirical propriety of conceiving that two different texts can carry identical communicative intentions.

claims.text: Efficiency in prose must be measured relative to particular semantic intentions, not in an absolute sense.
claims.source_passage: Those who dislike the word efficiency should realize that I use it relative to particular semantic intentions, not in an absolute sense.

claims.text: The primary communicative purpose of legal statutes is certainty of understanding rather than ease of reading.
claims.source_passage: This point about the relativity of readability is most strikingly evidenced in the prose of legal statutes. Their main communicative purpose is to make their semantic intentions understood with certainty rather than with ease.

claims.text: Legal drafting requires the avoidance of grammatical ambiguities and pronouns at the expense of 'pretty writing.'
claims.source_passage: He must avoid all graces, not be afraid of repetitions... he must eschew all pronouns when their antecedents might possibly be open to dispute, and generally avoid every potential grammatical ambiguity.... No one can expect pretty writing from anyone thus burdened.

claims.text: Medieval authors placed a low priority on creating devices to increase prose readability.
claims.source_passage: The self-conscious creation of devices to increase the readability of prose would have been a rather low-priority goal of medieval authors.

claims.text: In the medieval period, the inconsistent spelling of vernacular languages made reading a slow, puzzle-like process.
claims.source_passage: The medieval reader of a vernacular text could not quickly be sure from the mere figures on the manuscript just which word he confronted, so variable were the spellings of words in the vernacular languages.

claims.text: Highly literate medieval individuals typically muttered the sounds of text aloud while reading.
claims.source_passage: The reading process was slowed down still more by the well-attested habit of even the most highly literate persons in those days of muttering the sounds aloud to themselves.

claims.text: Increasing the readability of prose was a low-priority goal for medieval authors.
claims.source_passage: The self-conscious creation of devices to increase the readability of prose would have been a rather low-priority goal of medieval authors.

claims.text: Before the wide dissemination of print, the decipherment of writing was a time-consuming puzzle regardless of the writer's style.
claims.source_passage: Before the wide dissemination of written speech in print, the decipherment of writing was a time-consuming puzzle no matter how excellent the writer’s style.

claims.text: The high variability of vernacular spelling in medieval manuscripts prevented readers from quickly identifying words.
claims.source_passage: The medieval reader of a vernacular text could not quickly be sure from the mere figures on the manuscript just which word he confronted, so variable were the spellings of words in the vernacular languages.

claims.text: In antiquity and the medieval period, reading was normally a slow process involving muttering sounds aloud.
claims.source_passage: The reading process was slowed down still more by the well-attested habit of even the most highly literate persons in those days of muttering the sounds aloud to themselves.

claims.text: The virtues of stylistic efficiency only became urgent once reading became a primarily visual and rapid process rather than a vocalized one.
claims.source_passage: Only after reading became a preponderantly visual, and a potentially more rapid, process than muttered monologue, would the virtues of stylistic efficiency in prose have presented themselves urgently to the minds of authors and readers.

claims.text: Normalized spelling is an absolute prerequisite for rapid visual reading.
claims.source_passage: it is significant that the rise of utilitarian prose in the late seventeenth century coincided with the establishment of a normalized spelling—which is, of course, an absolute prerequisite for rapid visual reading.

claims.text: The communicative efficiency of printed discourse is primarily determined by prose style once spelling and typography are standardized.
claims.source_passage: Prose style then became the only significant factor in the communicative efficiency of printed discourse.

claims.text: The psychology of communication contains psycholinguistic universals that remain invariant across all times and places.
claims.source_passage: The psychology of communication, particularly as instanced in the work of George A. Miller, has yielded certain psycholinguistic universals which remain invariant in all times and places.

claims.text: Human channel capacity for making unidimensional judgments is limited to a range of about four to ten categories.
claims.source_passage: There seems to be some limitation built into us either by learning or by the design of our nervous system, a limit that keeps our channel capacity in this general range [four to ten categories].

claims.text: Human short-term memory cannot hold more than about seven random units.
claims.source_passage: Second, we cannot hold in our short-term memory more than about seven random units.

claims.text: In reading, if disconnected items exceed the human discrimination limit, readers must reread to regroup the items.
claims.source_passage: In reading, if the disconnected items we need to discriminate are numerous, we will make mistakes in reading the phrase or sentence, and we will have to reread it until the items are regrouped into a smaller number of items.

claims.text: The limitations of short-term memory are independent of cultural influence and apply to all languages and historical periods.
claims.source_passage: Here again, the constants of the human mind are independent of cultural influence. ... And this will be true no matter what language we are reading, or what period we live in.

claims.text: Words in a sentence are easier to recall than random words because they group into familiar units.
claims.source_passage: A sequence of twenty-five words in a sentence is easier to recall than a sequence of twenty-five words taken haphazardly from the dictionary. The sentence is easier because the words group themselves easily into familiar units.

claims.text: Human memory begins to fail when the number of items in a sequence exceeds six or seven.
claims.source_passage: in all these cases, people begin to make mistakes of memory when the number of items exceeds six or seven.

claims.text: If a reading sequence exceeds seven units before resolving into a pattern, readers must normally backtrack to check the information.
claims.source_passage: If, in reading, we encounter a series of units which climbs above seven before being resolved in a definite pattern, we will not normally remember all the units held in suspension. We will have to go back to check up.

claims.text: The memory constraints regarding reading units are universal across all languages and historical periods.
claims.source_passage: And this will be true no matter what language we are reading, or what period we live in.

claims.text: The word is an inappropriate unit for measuring the psychological length of a sentence because sentences are easier to recall than random word lists.
claims.source_passage: The sentence is easier because the words group themselves easily into familiar units. In terms of psychological units, a twenty-five word sentence is shorter than a sequence of twenty-five unrelated words. This means that the word is not the appropriate unit for measuring the psychological length of a sentence.

claims.text: The appropriate unit for judging the psychological length of prose is the unit of semantic closure.
claims.source_passage: The appropriate unit for judging the psychological length of phrases and sentences is the unit of semantic closure.

claims.text: Modern readers likely possess more fully developed linguistic unitizing abilities than readers from the sixteenth century.
claims.source_passage: But history itself suggests that the linguistic, unitizing abilities of a typical modern reader are more fully developed than those of a typical sixteenth-century reader.

claims.text: Shakespeare's prose style is more readable because it fulfills reader expectations and resolves syntactic groups quickly.
claims.source_passage: Shakespeare's prose fulfills expectations and quickly resolves syntactic groups.

claims.text: Ben Jonson committed a communicative error by modeling his written discourse on oral conversation.
claims.source_passage: Jonson’s style deliberately baffles expectations and makes the communicative error of modeling written discourse on oral conversation.

claims.text: The special demands of written speech are inherently difficult to grasp.
claims.source_passage: the special demands of written speech are inherently difficult to grasp.

claims.text: Modern students struggle with writing because their primary exposure is to oral speech, placing them in a historical position similar to sixteenth-century authors.
claims.source_passage: And in our own day, Johnny’s exposure mainly to oral speech places him in a position analogous to that of many sixteenth-century authors like Jonson, whose instincts for prose happened to be less genial and prefigurative than those of Sidney or Shakespeare.

claims.text: Effective scribal norms for prose had not been clearly established during the early seventeenth century.
claims.source_passage: The most effective scribal norms had not been clearly established in Jonson’s day.

claims.text: Jonson's and Milton's poetry is more effortlessly understood than their prose.
claims.source_passage: to determine that sense requires proportionately more time and effort from the reader than does the poem on Shakespeare. A very similar contrast is observed between the poetry and prose of Milton.

claims.text: Ben Jonson's prose requires proportionately more time and effort from the reader than his poetry.
claims.source_passage: That passage does make good sense, as do all of Jonson’s writings, but to determine that sense requires proportionately more time and effort from the reader than does the poem on Shakespeare.

claims.text: John Milton's prose is less readable than his poetry.
claims.source_passage: A very similar contrast is observed between the poetry and prose of Milton.

claims.text: Prose can approach poetry in readability during earlier periods, as evidenced by Sidney, Shakespeare, and Chaucer.
claims.source_passage: It is improbable to argue that they could not write readable prose; others, like Sidney and Shakespeare, and even Chaucer, show that prose can approach poetry in readability during the earlier periods.

claims.text: Meter and rhyme enhance readability due to the constraints of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: But, without doubt, meter and rhyme enhance readability for reasons which hark back to Professor Miller’s magic numbers.

claims.text: In pentameter and tetrameter verse, single lines are short enough to be fully held in short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: In pentameter and tetrameter verse, the single verse-lines are short enough to be fully held in short-term memory.

claims.text: Verse normally achieves syntactic-semantic closure within one or at most two lines.
claims.source_passage: In verse, the normal tendency is to achieve some degree of syntactic-semantic closure within each individual line, or at most within the space of two verse lines.

claims.text: The temporal falling into place of verbal beats in poetry achieves a sense of closure even without full explicit understanding.
claims.source_passage: The temporal falling into place of the verbal beats achieves a sense of closure even in the absence of fully explicit understanding.

claims.text: Metrical poetry possesses built-in expectation fulfillments that are absent in unrhythmical prose.
claims.source_passage: Metrical poetry has built-in expectation fulfillments absent in unrhythmical prose.

claims.text: By the eighteenth century, the readability of prose caught up with the readability of poetry.
claims.source_passage: In the eighteenth century, such contrasts between poetry and prose will become ever more difficult to draw. By then, prose will have caught up with poetry in readability

claims.text: The Spectator reached a standard of relative readability that could scarcely be improved upon once English became a normalized grapholect.
claims.source_passage: The Spectator, as Samuel Johnson recognized, represented a standard of relative readability which (English having become a normalized grapholect) could scarcely be improved upon.

claims.text: The general evolution of language and prose towards readability is irreversible.
claims.source_passage: In this chapter I have been concerned to suggest the irreversibility of this general evolution towards readability.

claims.text: Languages possess a universal tendency to move towards greater communicative efficiency.
claims.source_passage: I have cited the universal tendency of languages to move towards greater communicative efficiency, and have claimed that, on the large view, prose has also exhibited this evolutionary tendency.

claims.text: Historical progress in readability was achieved by developing prose conventions that set up normalized patterns of expectation.
claims.source_passage: This historical progress was achieved primarily by developing prose conventions which set up ever more normalized patterns of expectation.

claims.text: Improving readability required shortening the stretches of discourse that remain unresolved so they can be held in short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: Progress in readability also required a shortening of unresolved stretches of discourse, so that the reader could hold the words in short-term memory until he achieved some degree of semantic-syntactic closure.

claims.text: Psycholinguistic features of readability provide an authentic foundation for the contemporary teaching of composition.
claims.source_passage: Now I shall take leave of historical speculation to examine how these psycholinguistic features still continue to determine the readability of prose, and provide in our own day an authentic foundation for the teaching of composition.

claims.text: The nineteenth-century linguistic model posited a pattern of early, gradually achieved perfection followed by a gradual decline and degeneration.
claims.source_passage: In the nineteenth century one popular model was the pattern of an early, gradually achieved perfection followed by a gradual decline: the Greek of the Periclean Age or the Latin of the Augustan Age marked a high point which was followed by slow degeneration.

claims.text: The twentieth-century view that every stage of a language is inherently equal is the historical corollary to the sociolinguistic doctrine of the equality of dialects.
claims.source_passage: In the twentieth century, historical linguists have inclined to a more neutral mode of description which views any one stage of a language as being inherently equal, as a linguistic system, to any other stage in its history... This is the historical corollary to the doctrine of the equality of dialects.

claims.text: Language change is governed by the 'Principle of Least Effort,' which establishes an inverse relationship between linguistic complexity and frequency of use.
claims.source_passage: Zipf, moreover, has assembled an impressive amount of statistical data from several languages to back up what he calls 'The principle of Least Effort.' Probably his most impressive data for this principle are those which establish beyond doubt an inverse relationship between linguistic complexity and frequency of use.

claims.text: At any randomly chosen point in a modern English sentence, there are, on average, approximately ten grammatical and meaningful lexical choices available.
claims.source_passage: Recently it has been estimated that the total number of semantically probable lexical choices at any point in a modern English sentence—from the first word to the final period—averages out to ten.

claims.text: Syntactic and lexical techniques in prose are specifically invented to supply meaning clues that are otherwise provided by the 'interpersonal context' in oral speech.
claims.source_passage: the early sixteenth-century practitioners would need to invent special syntactic and lexical techniques which would help supply clues to meaning in the absence of an interpersonal context.

claims.text: Legal draftsmen must use strings of near-synonyms to amplify words with a small penumbra and specific definitions to limit words with a large penumbra.
claims.source_passage: he must limit by definition words with a penumbra dangerously large, and amplify with a string of near-synonyms words with a penumbra dangerously small

claims.text: Human channel capacity for making unidimensional judgments is invariant across different sensory attributes, including pitch, loudness, saltiness, and color brightness.
claims.source_passage: The universality of this human limitation is underscored by the evidence from both cultural and noncultural experience. For example, channel capacity is limited in about the same way for (1) tones of different pitch, (2) sounds of different loudness, (3) salt solutions of different strength...

claims.text: The decision to use communicative efficiency as a criterion for judging prose depends on whether efficiency addresses the specific question an evaluator wishes to answer.
claims.source_passage: The decision to apply it must rest in part upon whether communicative efficiency names a question to which one desires an answer.

claims.text: The 'interposition of self-conscious craft' in writing allows authors to preserve and use syntactic forms that are defunct in contemporary oral speech.
claims.source_passage: The interposition of self-conscious craft in writing makes the history of prose much more difficult to manage than the general history of language... the authority of such canonical writers as Jonson and Shakespeare will encourage later writers to use syntactic devices which may have become quite defunct in the oral speech of their own time.

evidence.description: Historical record of Indo-European languages showing evolution toward shorter, more regular, and more abstract forms.
evidence.source_reference: Jespersen, Progress in Language (1894), Language (1922), Efficiency in Language Change (1941)

evidence.description: The thousand-year evolution of English grammar from King Alfred to the present, focusing on the loss of inflections.
evidence.source_reference: Henry Bradley, cited by Hirsch

evidence.description: The redundant Latin construction 'virorum omnorum honorum' compared to more efficient later forms.
evidence.source_reference: Jespersen

evidence.description: The evolution of English inflections into consonantal suffixes (like the plural 's') which do not add syllables.
The shortening of the word 'television' to 'TV' or 'telly' due to frequent use.
Zipf's statistical data demonstrating the inverse relationship between complexity and frequency of use across several languages.
evidence.source_reference: G. K. Zipf, 'Relative Frequency as a Determinant of Phonetic Change', 1929; The Psychobiology of Language, 1935

evidence.description: Scholarly consensus that a major shift in prose occurred in the late 17th century.
The observation that some 19th-century writers appear more old-fashioned and difficult than early 18th-century writers.
Visser's comprehensive survey fails to show significant syntactic discontinuity (deaths/births) in English over five centuries.
evidence.source_reference: F. Th. Visser, An Historical Syntax of the English Language (1963-)

evidence.description: A comparison between Shakespeare and Ben Jonson shows that adventurous writers utilize rare syntactic techniques that are preserved by their authority.
evidence.source_reference: J. A. Barish, Ben Jonson and the Language of Prose Comedy (1960)

evidence.description: The average number of semantically probable lexical choices at any point in a modern English sentence is estimated at ten.
evidence.source_reference: Not explicitly named in text (cited as note 13)

evidence.description: Calculated average of grammatical continuations available at any given moment in speech/text (approximately ten).
evidence.source_reference: G. A. Miller, The Psychology of Communication (1970)
evidence.source_passage: If you interupt a speaker at some randomly chosen instant, there will be, on the average, about ten words that form grammatical and meaningful continuations.

evidence.description: Inference regarding Ben Jonson's attempts to use conversational models for prose and their relative effectiveness.
evidence.source_reference: Barish, Ben Jonson and the Language of Prose Comedy
evidence.source_passage: This is an inference that Barish draws in his discussion of Ben Jonson’s attempt to be conversational

evidence.description: A comparative analysis of English translations of Boccaccio's Decameron from 1566, 1620, and the 18th century, showing a trend toward greater clarity and efficiency.
evidence.source_reference: Painter, Palace of Pleasure (1566); 1620 Anonymous Translation; 18th Century Translation

evidence.description: Psycholinguistic testing methods involving eye-movement tracking and control groups to measure readability.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch references 'psycholinguistic testing of readability' to be discussed later

evidence.description: Analysis of the phrase 'the same from base estate advanced him to be Sultan of Babylon' showing syntactic ambiguity.
The presence of three long participial clauses preceding the main clause in the 1620 translation of The Decameron.
Comparison of 'not only... and also... likewise' in 1620 versus modern 'not only... but also' conventions.
Comparison of phrases describing 'victories' to show the evolution of parallel forms and proleptic 'both'.
The specific functional requirements of legal drafting as a counter-example to 'ease' being the sole measure of efficiency.
evidence.source_reference: E. Gowers, The Complete Plain Words (London, 1954), p. 9
evidence.source_passage: It is accordingly the duty of a draftsman of these authoritative texts to try to imagine every possible combination of circumstances to which his words might apply... he must eschew all pronouns when their antecedents might possibly be open to dispute

evidence.description: The historical variability of vernacular spelling in medieval manuscripts.
Historical evidence of the habit of reading aloud (vocalizing) among literate people in the Middle Ages.
A specific scene in Grimmelshausen's Simplicissimus describing a character's reaction to the act of reading.
evidence.source_reference: Grimmelshausen, Simplicissimus (Bk. 1, chap. 10)

evidence.description: Literary depiction of a character (a hermit) reading the Bible aloud to himself, being mistaken for having a conversation.
evidence.source_reference: Grimmelshausen, Simplicissimus, Bk. 1, chap. 10

evidence.description: Historical evidence showing that vocalized reading was the norm in antiquity and early Christianity.
evidence.source_reference: H. J. Chaytor, From Script to Print (1967)

evidence.description: The coincidence of the rise of utilitarian prose with the standardization of spelling in the late 17th century.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (historical observation)

evidence.description: Experiments showing human channel capacity is limited similarly across various sensory inputs (tones, loudness, salt solutions, size, color, pressure).
evidence.source_reference: George A. Miller, The Psychology of Communication

evidence.description: Memory tests showing mistake rates increase when sequences of random items (digits, letters, words) exceed seven units.
evidence.source_reference: George A. Miller, The Psychology of Communication

evidence.description: George Miller's psychological research on the limits of short-term memory and the grouping of items into units.
evidence.source_reference: George Miller, The Psychology of Communication, pp. 19-20, 31

evidence.description: Jonas Barish's comparative analysis of Shakespeare's and Jonson's prose styles and their relative readability.
evidence.source_reference: Jonas Barish, Ben Jonson and the Language of Prose Comedy, pp. 45-77

evidence.description: A direct comparison of Jonson's poem 'To the Reader' (on Shakespeare's portrait) and his prose preface to 'Sejanus.'
evidence.source_reference: Ben Jonson, Sejanus (1605) and 'On the Portrait of Shakespeare'

evidence.description: A comparison between the stylistic clarity of Milton's 'Lycidas' (poetry) and his 'Areopagitica' (prose).
evidence.source_reference: John Milton, Lycidas (1637) and Areopagitica (1644)

evidence.description: Miller's 'magic numbers' regarding short-term memory limits as a basis for the readability of short verse lines.
evidence.source_reference: George Miller (implicit reference to 'The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two', 1956)

evidence.description: The prose of 'The Spectator' as an example of normalized English grapholect achieving peak relative readability.
The ability of early writers like Sidney, Shakespeare, and Chaucer to write readable prose shows readability isn't purely a chronological lack of skill.

concepts.author_definition: The theory that language evolution is not neutral or degenerative but moves forward toward greater communicative functionality and efficiency.
concepts.common_alternative: Nineteenth-century view of degeneration from a classical 'perfect' state, or the twentieth-century 'neutral' view that all language stages are equal.
concepts.source_passage: Other twentieth-century linguists, most notably Jespersen, and most recently Martinet, hold the view that language change is on the whole progressive.

concepts.author_definition: The achievement of maximum effect in the hearer with a minimum of effort (muscular, temporal, or cognitive) on the part of the speaker/writer.
The process by which more efficient linguistic forms are slowly developed and codified over time rather than emerging fully formed.
concepts.source_passage: it is highly reasonable to conceive of linguistic change as governed by a process of trial and error.

concepts.author_definition: The capacity of a language to express complex meanings using minimal syllables or shorter forms, often achieved through the reduction of inflections.
The psychological and linguistic law that humans naturally seek to minimize time and cognitive/physical effort to achieve a communicative effect.
concepts.source_passage: Zipf... has assembled an impressive amount of statistical data from several languages to back up what he calls 'The principle of Least Effort.'

concepts.author_definition: Grammatical agreements (like Latin's 'virorum omnorum honorum') that repeat information; historically useful for meaning but discarded once efficiency is prioritized.
The historical process in grapholects where the variety of syntactic patterns and choices in word order are reduced to a more regular, predictable set.
The deliberate, artistic manipulation of language by writers (like Jonson or Shakespeare) that creates anomalies in the history of prose which do not reflect general linguistic trends.
A standard of prose quality based on how efficiently meaning is conveyed in the absence of interpersonal, situational contexts.
The inherent difficulty of writing caused by the lack of an immediate audience and the resulting absence of situational cues to meaning.
The historical trend where diverse stylistic and syntactic devices are replaced by a more regularized set of effective techniques.
The method of predicting historical linguistic trends based on theoretical principles of readability before consulting empirical data.
A psychological state in which a reader must hold unresolved modifications and grammatical relationships in mind before the main clause or meaning becomes clear.
Syntactic conventions (e.g., 'not only', 'both', 'from') that signal to the reader what kind of grammatical structure or temporal sequence is coming, thereby fulfilling expectations.
The view that all linguistic stages or dialects are equally valid and efficient, which Hirsch opposes by arguing for progressive readability.
Constants in the operation of the human mind that remain stable across different historical periods and cultures, allowing for objective comparisons of cognitive tasks like reading.
The specific meaning or effect a writer intends to convey, which dictates the appropriate standard for measuring readability.
The historical habit of vocalizing text while reading, which makes reading a slower, auditory-dependent process.
A standardized system of orthography that is a prerequisite for rapid visual reading and communicative efficiency.
A finite, built-in human limit (generally four to ten categories) for making unidimensional judgments or discriminating between sequence items.
The 'mnemonic units' of thought, which in prose are not necessarily individual words but can be 'familiar units' or groups that reduce cognitive load.
The span of text required for a reader to resolve a syntactic or meaningful pattern; the primary metric for psychological sentence length.
The psychological process by which several individual words are grouped into a single familiar unit, reducing the load on short-term memory.
The established, evolved conventions of written language that facilitate efficient communication and differ from oral patterns.
The point in a sentence or verse where both the grammatical structure and the intended meaning are fully resolved for the reader.
Prose conventions that allow a reader to accurately anticipate the linguistic and semantic direction of a text, reducing cognitive effort.
A standard of prose excellence defined by the communication of intended meaning with the least possible reader effort, relative to the complexity of the subject.
A standard written language that has reached a stable, conventionalized state, facilitating maximum communicative efficiency.
A standard written language that has been developed over time and transcends regional dialects.
The human capacity to discriminate between items along a single sensory or cognitive scale (e.g., pitch, size, or category).
The situational and social cues present in face-to-face oral communication that are absent in written monologue.
The range or 'shadow' of potential meanings and interpretations surrounding a specific word.
The groupings (such as unitized phrases) into which information is organized in short-term memory, as opposed to individual words.

cases.name: English from King Alfred to the Modern Era
cases.description: The development of English over a thousand-year period characterized by the acquisition of new grammatical resources and the disappearance of superfluous inflections, leading to greater capacity for condensation and efficiency.
key_passages: Addressing the question whether the changes in English since the days of King Alfred have enhanced “the efficiency of the language as an instrument of expression,” Bradley states: “We have already pointed out the great value of some of the additions which the language has made to its grammatical resources during the last thousand years.

cases.name: Indo-European language evolution
cases.description: The broad historical movement of Indo-European languages from complex, redundant systems toward shorter, more regular, and analytic structures.
key_passages: In comparing the early and later stages of Indo-European languages one always finds increased functionality

cases.name: English Linguistic History
cases.description: The historical development of English from a more inflected language to one characterized by 'vigorous condensation' and efficiency. It serves as a positive example of the universal trend toward grammatical simplification.
key_passages: English has gained in efficiency as a means of expression. The disappearance of superfluous inflections... have greatly increased the capacity of the language for vigorous condensation.

cases.name: English Prose History vs. Language History
cases.description: A comparison between the 'orderly' linear history of the English language (studied statistically) and the 'wavy, uncertain' history of English prose (studied via individual texts).
key_passages: The history of prose style appears to form a wavy, uncertain pattern, unlike the more linear course of the English language as a whole.

cases.name: English Syntax 16th-20th Century
cases.description: The author examines the continuity of English syntax over five centuries, finding that despite the 'disarray' of individual styles, the underlying syntactic structures show remarkable stability and a trend toward normalization.
key_passages: I have not been able to find a single clear-cut example of such discontinuity—a result which at least suggests that syntactic deaths and births must be exceedingly rare over the past five centuries.

cases.name: English Prose 16th-18th Century
cases.description: A historical progression where prose moved from diverse, experimental, and varied models (including oral and foreign language models) toward a more uniform, regularized, and efficient standard by the 18th century.
key_passages: In every sixteenth-century grapholect of Europe, we will probably find great variations in the effectiveness of prose and great diversity in the syntactic and lexical devices on which sentences are built. By the eighteenth century, we should expect to find much more uniformity of practice.
contestable_aspects: The assumption that 18th-century prose is objectively 'more efficient' than 16th-century prose.
The characterization of early prose writers as 'misguided' if they chose conversational models.

cases.name: Boccaccio's Decameron Translations
cases.description: A 'simple experiment' comparing the first two sentences of The Decameron across three centuries of English translation (1566, 1620, 1700s). The comparison illustrates a clear reduction in cognitive load and syntactic ambiguity.
key_passages: I decided to seek a work other than the Bible which, like the Bible, had been often translated into English.
Most readers will instead consult their own subjective experience of the passages... for grasping the progressive reduction of time and effort required to read the three passages.

cases.name: William Painter's Decameron (1566)
cases.description: The earliest version analyzed, characterized by lexical inefficiencies and syntactic choices that separate subjects and verbs, creating longer periods of uncertainty for the reader.
key_passages: his phrase: the same from base estate advanced him to be Sultan of Babylon could be reordered in a variety of ways.
the original placement of the phrase from base estate occasions another difficulty... by separating the subject and verb and inserting a not-yet-resolved modification between them

cases.name: 1620 Decameron Translation
cases.description: A transitional version that removes some lexical inefficiency but retains syntactic burdens, such as multiple long participial clauses preceding the main clause.
key_passages: The syntactic inefficiency in Painter is almost entirely absent in the version of 1620, where the imposition of effort on the reader is to be found mainly in the length of unresolved syntactic-semantic units.
one of the participial phrases is itself interrupted by its own participial modification: (by reason of some sudden accident happening to him).

cases.name: Legal Statutes
cases.description: Hirsch uses the example of legal drafting to demonstrate 'relative readability.' In legal contexts, the intention is to avoid any possible misinterpretation. This requires repetitions, definitions of terms with large 'penumbras,' and the avoidance of pronouns. While this makes the prose 'ugly' and hard to read for a layperson, it is 'efficient' for its specific purpose of legal certainty.
key_passages: Their main communicative purpose is to make their semantic intentions understood with certainty rather than with ease.
He must avoid all graces, not be afraid of repetitions... he must eschew all pronouns when their antecedents might possibly be open to dispute

cases.name: Medieval Vernacular Reading
cases.description: Before the era of print and standardized spelling, the act of reading was a 'time-consuming puzzle.' Hirsch argues that because the physical act of deciphering variable spellings and the habit of reading aloud were so laborious, authors had little incentive to refine stylistic readability.
key_passages: the decipherment of writing was a time-consuming puzzle no matter how excellent the writer’s style.
variable were the spellings of words in the vernacular languages.
the well-attested habit of even the most highly literate persons in those days of muttering the sounds aloud to themselves.

cases.name: Late Seventeenth Century English Prose
cases.description: The era marking the rise of utilitarian prose, which Hirsch argues was made possible by the stabilization of spelling, enabling rapid visual processing.
key_passages: it is significant that the rise of utilitarian prose in the late seventeenth century coincided with the establishment of a normalized spelling

cases.name: Ben Jonson's Prose vs. Poetry
cases.description: Jonson is presented as a writer who excelled in poetry but struggled with prose because he modeled the latter on oral speech before clear written (scribal) norms were established. His prose in 'Sejanus' is cited as requiring significant effort compared to his 'To the Reader' poem.
key_passages: Jonson’s style deliberately baffles expectations and makes the communicative error of modeling written discourse on oral conversation.
I believe anyone can understand that elegant and gracious poem more effortlessly than the following address to the reader which prefaces Jonson’s play Sejanus
contestable_aspects: The characterization of Jonson's prose as an 'error' rather than a deliberate stylistic choice for aesthetic or generic reasons.

cases.name: The Modern Student ('Johnny')
cases.description: The author compares modern students who struggle with writing to early 17th-century writers. The claim is that both groups suffer from a lack of exposure to or establishment of the specific norms of context-free written speech, relying instead on oral instincts.
key_passages: Johnny’s exposure mainly to oral speech places him in a position analogous to that of many sixteenth-century authors like Jonson

cases.name: 17th-Century Prose vs. Poetry (Jonson and Milton)
cases.description: The author contrasts the prose and poetry of Ben Jonson and John Milton, arguing that their prose is significantly less readable despite their mastery of verse. This demonstrates that prose conventions for readability lagged behind the structural advantages already present in metrical verse (short lines, rhythm, and rhyme).
key_passages: Why should Jonson and Milton write prose that is less readable than their poetry?
That passage does make good sense... but to determine that sense requires proportionately more time and effort from the reader than does the poem on Shakespeare.

cases.name: The Eighteenth-Century Readability Shift
cases.description: The author identifies the 18th century, and specifically the publication of 'The Spectator', as the era when English prose caught up to poetry in terms of readability, achieving a 'normalized grapholect' that maximized communicative efficiency.
key_passages: In the eighteenth century, such contrasts between poetry and prose will become ever more difficult to draw. By then, prose will have caught up with poetry in readability
The Spectator... represented a standard of relative readability which (English having become a normalized grapholect) could scarcely be improved upon.

thinkers.name: Otto Jespersen
thinkers.key_idea: Language change is progressive and moves toward communicative efficiency (maximum effect with minimum effort).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch adopts Jespersen's efficiency principle as the foundation for his own theory of relative readability and prose history.
key_passages: “the best is what with a minimum of effort on the part of the speaker produces a maximum of effect in the hearer.” According to Jespersen, the tendency of language change has been on the whole in this direction.

thinkers.name: Henry Bradley
thinkers.key_idea: The historical development of English has improved its efficiency as an instrument of expression through the loss of inflections.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Bradley's work to provide specific historical support for the efficiency thesis within the English grapholect.
key_passages: With particular respect to English grammar, Jespersen’s view is vigorously supported by Henry Bradley.

thinkers.name: André Martinet
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Martinet as a recent proponent of the progressive view to show its continued relevance in 20th-century linguistics.
key_passages: most recently Martinet, hold the view that language change is on the whole progressive.

thinkers.name: George Kingsley Zipf
thinkers.key_idea: The Principle of Least Effort and the inverse relationship between word frequency and length.
thinkers.source_work: Human Behavior and the Principle of Least Effort (1949)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Zipf's statistical data as empirical proof for the psychological mechanisms Jespersen theorized.
key_passages: Zipf... has assembled an impressive amount of statistical data from several languages to back up what he calls 'The principle of Least Effort.'

thinkers.name: R. W. Chambers
thinkers.key_idea: Proposes a theory of continuity in the structure of English prose from earliest times.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Chambers to illustrate the lack of consensus and the 'scholarly disarray' in prose history studies.
key_passages: R. W. Chambers finds continuity in the structure of English prose from earliest times, an opinion from which Norman Davis vigorously dissents.

thinkers.name: Jonas Barish
thinkers.key_idea: The difference between the prose styles of Jonson and Shakespeare highlights how writers experiment with rare syntactic techniques.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Barish's comparison to explain why the history of prose is more complex and less orderly than the history of language.
key_passages: This has been done very ably by Jonas Barish, and what emerges from his comparison is the realization that adventurous writers will try out a very great number of syntactic techniques even if these are rare and strange.

thinkers.name: F. Th. Visser
thinkers.key_idea: A comprehensive historical mapping of English syntax.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch relies on Visser's massive work as the empirical basis for his claim that English syntax has not changed significantly in terms of basic patterns over 500 years.
key_passages: To prove this point, one need only consult Visser’s monumental, multivolumed Historical Syntax of the English Language

thinkers.name: George A. Miller
thinkers.key_idea: The psychological limit of expectancy in speech: at any point, there are on average only ten grammatical/meaningful ways to continue a sentence.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Miller's psychological data to support the idea that reducing syntactic uncertainty (normalization) directly increases efficiency.
key_passages: This has been achieved in our language by the normalization of syntax... G. A. Miller, The Psychology of Communication

thinkers.name: William Painter
thinkers.key_idea: Syntactic structures and lexical choices used in his 1566 translation of Boccaccio.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Painter as a baseline for 16th-century prose, analyzing his 'marginal inefficiencies' and 'syntactic techniques' as examples of what history eventually optimized.
key_passages: Painter’s Palace of Pleasure of 1566, a source book for some of Shakespeare’s plots.
We are not concerned at all to condemn Painter’s style but merely to observe why some of his devices became obsolete by as early as 1620.

thinkers.name: Giovanni Boccaccio
thinkers.key_idea: The original Italian source of the Decameron text used for the translation comparison.
thinkers.engagement: Boccaccio provides the semantic constant (the 'same meaning') that allows Hirsch to compare the efficiency of different English syntactic realizations.
key_passages: The work that occurred to me was The Decameron
The original Italian runs as follows: 'Il Saladino, il valore del qual fu tanto...'

thinkers.name: The Historicist
thinkers.key_idea: Readability is relative; only a reader from the same historical period as the text can judge its readability.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch acknowledges this as a reasonable-sounding objection based on linguistic expectations but intends to refute it using universal psychological principles.
key_passages: The historicist can quite reasonably object that the comparative readability of a translation will depend upon the linguistic expectations of readers, expectations which differ in different periods.

thinkers.name: Sir Ernest Gowers
thinkers.key_idea: The functional necessity of precision in legal drafting necessitates a style that eschews typical 'graces' of prose.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Gowers approvingly to demonstrate that 'efficiency' in prose is relative to the writer's goal (certainty vs. ease).
key_passages: Sir Ernest Gowers, in his sensible guide to efficient prose, makes that observation quite elegantly

thinkers.name: Grimmelshausen
thinkers.key_idea: An anecdotal observation of the reading process in the 17th century.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses a literary scene from Grimmelshausen to provide evidence for the historical nature of the reading process.
key_passages: In Grimmelshausen’s Simplicissimus (Bk. 1, chap. 10), the hero informs us: 'When I first saw him reading'

thinkers.name: H. J. Chaytor
thinkers.key_idea: The transition 'from script to print' and the evidence for oral/vocalized reading habits in antiquity.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Chaytor to establish the historical reality of how reading habits have changed, supporting the claim that medieval reading was fundamentally different from modern visual reading.
key_passages: Chaytor adduces a good deal of evidence that this was the normal way of reading in antiquity and early Christianity.

thinkers.name: Samuel Johnson
thinkers.key_idea: The Spectator as a standard of English prose.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Johnson to validate the claim that The Spectator achieved a near-perfect level of relative readability.
key_passages: The Spectator, as Samuel Johnson recognized, represented a standard of relative readability

actors.name: 19th-century philologists
actors.context: Proposed the model of linguistic decline from a 'perfect' classical age (Periclean Greek or Augustan Latin).

actors.name: 20th-century historical linguists
actors.context: Adopted a 'neutral' mode where every stage of language is inherently equal, a corollary to the doctrine of the equality of dialects.

actors.name: Otto Jespersen
actors.context: Linguist whose theories of linguistic efficiency support Hirsch's progressivist view of language.

actors.name: George Kingsley Zipf
actors.context: Statistician/linguist who provided empirical data for the Principle of Least Effort.

actors.name: Norman Davis
actors.context: Scholar who dissents from Chambers' view of prose continuity, used as an example of scholarly disagreement.

actors.name: Shakespeare
actors.context: Used as an example of a writer with a broader scope of syntactic choice and a canonical authority that preserves archaic forms.

actors.name: Ben Jonson
actors.context: Compared to Shakespeare to show the variety of syntactic experimentation in prose.

actors.name: Authorized Version of the Bible
actors.context: A canonical text that influences writers to maintain syntactic forms distinct from oral speech.

actors.name: Johannes Gutenberg
actors.context: His invention of the printing press is cited as the catalyst for the increased importance of prose and the expansion of the readership.

actors.name: William Shakespeare
actors.context: Cited as a point of comparison to illustrate that modern syntax has become more regular and manageable than the syntax of his era.

actors.name: William Painter
actors.context: Sixteenth-century translator whose prose style serves as a baseline for the analysis of linguistic progress.

actors.name: Psycholinguistic researchers
actors.context: The field that provides objective criteria (eye movements, control groups) for measuring prose efficiency.

actors.name: William Painter
actors.context: 16th-century translator of The Decameron whose prose serves as the baseline for syntactic inefficiency.

actors.name: Anonymous 1620 Translator
actors.context: Translator of the 1620 version of The Decameron used to show intermediate syntactic progress.

actors.name: Anonymous 1741 Translator
actors.context: 18th-century translator whose work demonstrates the normalization of modern English prose standards.

actors.name: The Historicist
actors.context: A hypothetical opponent who argues that readability is entirely relative to the linguistic expectations of a specific historical period.

actors.name: The Linguistic Egalitarian
actors.context: A hypothetical opponent who argues that communicative efficiency is an arbitrary criterion for judging the quality of prose.

actors.name: Sir Ernest Gowers
actors.context: Author of a guide to prose style used to explain the unique requirements of legal writing.

actors.name: George A. Miller
actors.context: Psychologist whose work on memory and channel capacity provides the 'constants' for Hirsch's theory.

actors.name: H. J. Chaytor
actors.context: Historian of literacy providing evidence for medieval reading habits.

actors.name: Grimmelshausen
actors.context: Author of Simplicissimus, used to provide a literary anecdote of vocalized reading.

actors.name: William Shakespeare
actors.context: Used as a positive example of early prose that anticipated modern standards of readability.

actors.name: John Milton
actors.context: Used to illustrate the discrepancy between the ease of poetry and the difficulty of early, unrefined prose.

actors.name: Ben Jonson
actors.context: Used as a negative example of prose style that failed to adapt to the requirements of written speech.

actors.name: Ben Jonson
actors.context: Cited as an example of a writer whose prose is significantly less readable than his poetry.

actors.name: John Milton
actors.context: Used to illustrate the readability gap between 17th-century verse (Lycidas) and prose (Areopagitica).

objections_raised.objection: The proliferation of many different languages from a single source (e.g., Indo-European) suggests language change is governed by contingency rather than universal principles.
objections_raised.response: Despite surface proliferation, an historical perspective reveals irresistible tendencies toward efficiency that transcend individual cultural contingencies.

objections_raised.objection: Languages reach a peak of 'perfection' in classical ages (like Augustan Latin) and then undergo slow degeneration.
objections_raised.response: The 'progressive' view (Jespersen/Martinet) is more accurate: change moves toward functionality, not away from a fixed point of perfection.

objections_raised.objection: All stages of a language are linguistically equal; no stage is 'better' than another.
objections_raised.response: While stages may be equal as 'systems,' they are not equal in 'communicative efficiency,' where later stages typically outperform earlier ones.

objections_raised.objection: The opinions that English has become more efficient might seem impressionistic or parochial.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch introduces the work of Jespersen, Zipf, and Martinet, showing that the tendency toward simplification is a universal fact backed by statistical data and information theory.

objections_raised.objection: Prose style does not show a clear linear progression; 19th-century prose can be more difficult than 18th-century prose.
objections_raised.response: The author suggests this is a result of looking at individual texts rather than a statistical overview of the prose 'grapholect' as a whole.

objections_raised.objection: The history of prose is disorderly only because scholars ask the wrong or imprecise questions.
objections_raised.response: The disorder is inherent in the subject itself because writers are self-conscious and canonical texts preserve defunct oral patterns, though a statistical 'Olympian' view can mitigate this.

objections_raised.objection: The claim that modern prose is more functional may be a result of cultural or historical bias.
objections_raised.response: To refute this, Hirsch argues the claim must be grounded in universal psychological absolutes that apply to all humans regardless of culture.

objections_raised.objection: Individual reader experience of reading ease is subjective and not a reliable guide to objective prose quality.
objections_raised.response: While subjective experience is not a 'sure guide,' it is useful for grasping the overall trend, and it can be validated by objective psycholinguistic testing involving eye movements and control groups.

objections_raised.objection: A translation's readability depends on the reader's historical era and linguistic expectations; therefore, modern readers cannot judge the readability of 16th-century prose.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch anticipates this objection (not fully answered in this snippet) but frames it as a challenge to his theory of universal principles of readability.

objections_raised.objection: The readability of a text can only be judged by readers of the era in which it was written because linguistic expectations change.
objections_raised.response: The existence of psychological universals (constants in the human mind) allows for cross-historical testing and comparison of cognitive processing across different eras.

objections_raised.objection: Using communicative efficiency as a benchmark for prose quality is an arbitrary value judgment.
objections_raised.response: While criteria are chosen based on purpose, efficiency reflects the actual historical evolution of speech, and most people naturally prefer to achieve goals with less effort.

objections_raised.objection: Skepticism regarding the validity of comparing prose styles across vastly different historical eras.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch argues that such comparison is valid because (1) common sense shows that stylistic efficiency became momentous once mechanical reading became easy, and (2) psycholinguistic constants of the human mind (memory and discrimination limits) are universal and invariant across all eras.

objections_raised.objection: The psychological units of discourse are not constant and vary significantly across different times and cultures.
objections_raised.response: While historical comparisons can be undecidable, the linguistic 'unitizing' abilities of modern readers appear more developed, and contemporary comparisons (like Shakespeare vs. Jonson) show that stylistic choices impact readability regardless of era.

objections_raised.objection: Are modern judgments of 17th-century prose as 'unreadable' simply anachronistic prejudices?
objections_raised.response: No, because the contrast is observed within the same authors (like Jonson and Milton) comparing their own prose to their own poetry; the poetry remains readable, suggesting the prose itself is technically less efficient.

cross_chapter_refs.reference_text: This difficulty, inherent in writing, I have discussed at some length in Chapter 1.
My answer to the historicist is mainly to be found in the next section, where I discuss some psychological constants
